header_summary:
The latest release candidate for Bitcoin Core, version v31.1rc1, is now available, offering preliminary insights into the updates and changes included in this minor version. Interested parties are encouraged to download and test the binaries from [Bitcoin Core's official website](https://bitcoincore.org/bin/bitcoin-core-31.1/test.rc1/) and review the source code on [GitHub](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/tree/v31.1rc1). Detailed release notes are accessible [here](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/blob/v31.1rc1/doc/release-notes.md), and users are urged to report any issues via the project's [GitHub issues page](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/issues).

A significant proposal within the Bitcoin community aims to introduce a perpetual block subsidy by setting an expiration on Unspent Transaction Outputs (UTXOs) to maintain the 21 million coin cap, ensuring miners continue receiving subsidies without surpassing the supply limit. This innovative approach, currently under discussion, involves transferring the nominal value of expired UTXOs to a pending emission reserve, with detailed documentation available on [GitHub](https://github.com/bip-proposer/bitcoin-infinity/blob/main/infinity.md).

The intersection of blockchain technology and artificial intelligence is gaining traction, with discussions focusing on using the Bitcoin blockchain for executing AI-driven contract protocols. Despite the complexity of integrating advanced cryptographic methods like functional homomorphic encryption, ongoing research and academic discussions, as highlighted in a recent paper available [here](https://dl.acm.org/doi/pdf/10.1145/3372297.3417278), continue to push the boundaries of secure and efficient blockchain applications.

Lastly, the evolving threat landscape for Bitcoin, particularly concerning miner consolidation and economic stability, remains a critical area of focus. Discussions suggest that even as block subsidies decrease, the community may consider drastic measures to maintain network integrity. The broader implications of such centralization and the potential market response to a more centralized Bitcoin model continue to be debated, reflecting ongoing uncertainty within the ecosystem.

recent_posts.title: Bitcoin Core v31.1rc1 is now available
recent_posts.summary:
- Bitcoin Core version v31.1rc1 is now available for testing and download.
- Users are encouraged to report any issues on the project's GitHub page.
- Feedback will determine the stability and advancement to the next stable release.

recent_posts.title: [BIP Proposal] Strict circular economy within the Bitcoin network
recent_posts.summary: - The proposal suggests a permanent block subsidy with a 21 million coin limit.  - UTXOs exceeding `MAX_UTXO_AGE` will be removed to ensure ongoing miner incentives.  - Expired satoshis are moved to a reserve to extend halving epochs, not recycled.

recent_posts.title: Accountable Computing On-Chain Contracts for AI Agents Supervision
recent_posts.summary: - The Bitcoin blockchain's role in AI contract protocols engages academic and practical interest.  - Real-world cryptography, especially functional homomorphic encryption, remains underdeveloped.  - Despite limited time, research on secure AI blockchain contracts indicates strong future potential.

recent_posts.title: Input-triggered transaction expiry
recent_posts.summary:
- An error in a proposed idea was identified and subsequently retracted.
- The error's recognition followed a correction from another party, prompting gratitude.
- This incident emphasizes the importance of peer review in collaborative problem-solving.

recent_posts.title: Addressing the Diminishing Block Subsidy
recent_posts.summary:
- The email addresses the evolving threat landscape for Bitcoin's security and stability.
- It suggests potential miner consolidation could control the network, despite not being an attack.
- The discussion also explores controversial measures to preserve network integrity in the face of threats.

recent_posts.title: Bitcoin Core v31.1rc1 is now available
recent_posts.summary:
- The latest Bitcoin Core release candidate, v31.1rc1, is now downloadable.
- Source code and binaries are accessible on GitHub and the Bitcoin Core website.
- Community feedback and testing are vital for the upcoming official release.

active_posts.title: Aligning privacy incentives in P2MR
active_posts.summary:
- The proposal modifies BIP360 by requiring a merkle path in P2MR transactions' control block.
- Aimed at removing depth-zero script trees, it mandates two spending conditions for P2MR addresses.
- The change seeks feedback, especially from P2TRv2 proponents, to evaluate its impact on privacy and fungibility.

active_posts.title: Prohibit Merkle Internal Node Preimages That Encode Minimal 64-Byte Transactions
active_posts.summary: - The proposal introduces a rule to invalidate Bitcoin blocks with certain 64-byte preimages.  - It targets transactions that could be confused with internal Merkle node structures.  - Implementation involves a C++ function to check and enforce this new rule during block construction.

active_posts.title: Giving teeth to expected EC disabling: P2XX(-T)(-ML)
active_posts.summary:
- Recent Bitcoin community discussions explore enhancing new output types to counter quantum threats.
- Two key disabling mechanisms proposed are Tripwire by Tadge Dryja and Miner Lockdown by Sjors Provoost.
- These proactive security measures align with the cryptocurrency's decentralized ethos and promote user involvement.

active_posts.title: State of the transaction privacy work in Bitcoin
active_posts.summary: - Bitcoin privacy focuses on sidechains, L2s, and wallet integrations, not on Layer 1.  - Cost-effective solutions like CISA may optimize but do not increase privacy.  - Current strategies prioritize regulatory compatibility and minor protocol adjustments.

active_posts.title: [BIP Draft] Segregated Data: a prunable, script-isolated block region for data carriage
active_posts.summary: - SegData introduces a new method for handling data within Bitcoin blocks via a soft-fork.  - It allows for prunable data storage, ensuring synchronization without long-term node retention.  - The proposal aims to benefit off-chain data interpreters and addresses implementation specifics.

active_posts.title: SHRINCS: 324-byte stateful post-quantum signatures with static backups
active_posts.summary:
- SHRINCS combines stateful and stateless signature schemes for robust, efficient operations.
- It defaults to stateless mode when state integrity is compromised, enhancing security.
- Detailed technical insights are available in Mikhail Kudinov's co-authored paper.

today_in_history_posts.title: BIP 151 use of HMAC_SHA512
today_in_history_posts.summary:
- Ethan Heilman criticizes the necessity of HMAC for MAC purposes.
- HMAC is favored over SHA256 for MACs due to proven security properties.
- SHA256's use is risky for MAC because of its length extension vulnerability.

today_in_history_posts.title: [BOLT Draft] Onion Routing Spec
today_in_history_posts.summary:
- Rusty Russell proposes restructuring the Lightning Network protocol for efficiency.
- His changes include consolidating data fields and allowing for future protocol extensions.
- He suggests improvements for node storage management and enhanced replay protection.

today_in_history_posts.title: bLIP: BOLT 11 Invoice Blinded Path Tagged Field
today_in_history_posts.summary:
- The proposal introduces a new BOLT 11 invoice field to enhance privacy in lightning payments.
- It includes technical specifications and compatibility strategies for implementation.
- The proposal seeks feedback to refine communication of path information and invoice integrity.
